questions.question: Explain Big O notation with a formal definition and give one example.
keyPoints: Formal: f(n) = O(g(n)) if ∃ c > 0 and n₀ such that 0 ≤ f(n) ≤ c×g(n) for all n ≥ n₀
Represents upper bound (worst-case)
Example: 3n² + 5n + 2 = O(n²) because for n ≥ 5, 3n²+5n+2 ≤ 4n²

questions.question: Differentiate between Big O, Big Omega, and Big Theta notations.
keyPoints: Big O (O): Upper bound - function grows no faster than g(n)
Big Omega (Ω): Lower bound - function grows at least as fast as g(n)
Big Theta (Θ): Tight bound - function grows at same rate as g(n)
When both O and Ω hold, we say Θ

questions.question: What is the time complexity of binary search? Derive its complexity.
keyPoints: Binary search: O(log n) time complexity
At each step, search space reduces from n to n/2
After k steps: n/2^k = 1, solving gives k = log₂n
Hence T(n) = O(log n)

questions.question: Explain the concept of best-case, worst-case, and average-case complexity with an example.
keyPoints: Best-case: Minimum resources needed (e.g., linear search O(1) when element is first)
Worst-case: Maximum resources needed (e.g., linear search O(n) when element is last)
Average-case: Expected resources over all inputs
Example: QuickSort - worst O(n²), average O(n log n)

questions.question: Arrange the following complexity classes in increasing order: O(n!), O(2ⁿ), O(n³), O(n log n), O(log n), O(n), O(1)
keyPoints: Correct order: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n³) < O(2ⁿ) < O(n!)
Constant < Logarithmic < Linear < Linearithmic < Cubic < Exponential < Factorial

questions.question: What is space complexity? How does it differ from time complexity?
keyPoints: Space complexity: Total memory required by algorithm as function of input size
Includes: Input memory + Auxiliary space (extra variables, data structures)
Time complexity: Number of basic operations performed
Space complexity: Amount of memory allocated
Both expressed in asymptotic notation

questions.question: Differentiate between Little o and Little ω notations with examples.
keyPoints: Little o (o): Strict upper bound - f(n) = o(g(n)) means f(n)/g(n) → 0 as n→∞
Example: n = o(n²) because n/n² = 1/n → 0
Little ω (ω): Strict lower bound - f(n) = ω(g(n)) means f(n)/g(n) → ∞ as n→∞
Example: n² = ω(n) because n²/n = n → ∞
Outer loop runs n times
Inner loop runs i times for each i
Total operations = 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + n = n(n+1)/2
Using formula: n(n+1)/2 = n²/2 + n/2
Therefore: Time complexity = O(n²) (dominant term)
Total primitive operations = Θ(n²)

questions.question: Explain the Merge Sort algorithm, derive its time complexity for best, worst, and average cases, and explain why it is preferred over QuickSort for certain applications.
keyPoints: Merge Sort: Divide array, recursively sort halves, merge sorted halves
Recurrence: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)
Best, Worst, Average all: Θ(n log n)
Stable sort - maintains relative order of equal elements
Predictable performance - no worst-case degradation
External sorting - works well with slow memory
However: O(n) space, not in-place

questions.question: Solve the recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n using Master Theorem and verify your answer.
keyPoints: Master Theorem: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) where a=2, b=2, f(n)=n
Compute n^(log_b a) = n^(log_2 2) = n^1 = n
Since f(n) = Θ(n^log_b a), this is Case 2
Therefore T(n) = Θ(n log n)
Verification: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n expands to n log n by recurrence tree
At each level: n work, number of levels: log n, Total: n log n

questions.question: What are the limitations of asymptotic analysis? Discuss with examples where asymptotic notation might not be the best metric for algorithm comparison.
keyPoints: Ignores constants - O(2n) vs O(n) might favor wrong algorithm for practical n
Does not account for cache performance (locality matters)
Ignores hardware differences
Small input sizes behave differently than asymptotic predictions
Space vs time tradeoffs not captured
Example: Algorithm A: O(n²) with small constant vs Algorithm B: O(n log n) with large constant
For small n, A might be faster despite worse complexity
Practical considerations: readability, maintainability, development time
